title: Writing JUnit Tests
description: Master JUnit testing framework by writing comprehensive unit tests with various assertion methods, test annotations (@Test, @Before, @After), and test lifecycle management. Learn to verify expected outcomes, handle exceptions, and organize test suites effectively.
instructions: Create a `Calculator` class with methods: add, subtract, multiply, divide
Create a `CalculatorTest` class with JUnit test methods
Implement @Before method `setUp()` to initialize a Calculator instance
Write test method `testAddition()` to verify add(5, 3) returns 8
Write test method `testSubtraction()` to verify subtract(10, 4) returns 6
Write test method `testMultiplication()` to verify multiply(6, 7) returns 42
Write test method `testDivision()` to verify divide(20, 4) returns 5
Write test method `testDivisionByZero()` to verify ArithmeticException is thrown
Use appropriate JUnit assertions: assertEquals, assertTrue, assertThrows
learningObjectives: Understand JUnit test structure and annotations
Master various assertion methods (assertEquals, assertTrue, assertFalse)
Understand the importance of test independence and isolation

hints.content: JUnit test methods must be public void and annotated with @Test. Use @Before to initialize objects before each test: 'calculator = new Calculator();'. This ensures test independence.
Use assertEquals(expected, actual) to verify results: 'assertEquals(8, calculator.add(5, 3));'. For exceptions, use @Test(expected = ExceptionClass.class) annotation or assertThrows in JUnit 5.

Complete solution: In divide method, check if b == 0 and throw new ArithmeticException("Cannot divide by zero"). Test it with @Test(expected = ArithmeticException.class) annotation. Each arithmetic operation should be a simple return statement with the operation.
